- Creates GZipStream to compress data, then checks if the output is empty (zero bytes) to detect "no data" scenarios
- Reads TAR archives that were hand-crafted by a legacy tool (some may have invalid checksums)
- Uses ZipArchive.CreateAsync for large ZIP files and relies on lazy loading behavior
- Uses MemoryStream with very large allocations close to the maximum capacity
What breaks when migrating to .NET 11?

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/46465:
### Description

`atime` and `ctime` are problematic in tar entries - not all tar readers support them. `GnuTarEntry` and `PaxTarEntry` have been changed to not add these fields by default. The fields will still be preserved when reading, and a user may set them directly, but they will not be set on existing entries that didn't have them to start, or when converting from other entry types.

The behavior `TarEntry.ModificationTime` is unchanged. It is initialized to `UtcNow` for tar entries created with a constructor, and uses the file modification time for entries created from files.

Other minor fixes have been made to `System.Formats.Tar` to prioritize round-tripping of `TarEntry`s from read -> write without change.

cc [@tmds](https://github.com/tmds)

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 5

### Previous behavior

`GnuTarEntry` and `PaxTarEntry` would always add `atime` and `ctime` values.

### New behavior

`GnuTarEntry` and `PaxTarEntry` only set `atime` and `ctime` if read from an entry that had them or the user explicitly set them.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Better compatibility with other tar readers, round-tripping tar files without modification.

### Recommended action

No action required for most users.  
If you require these fields to be set you can do so directly with `GnuTarEntry.AccessTime`, `GnuTarEntry.ChangeTime`, or `PaxTarEntry(TarEntryType entryType, string entryName, IEnumerable<KeyValuePair<string, string>> extendedAttributes)`\- but know that this will create a tar that is not readable by many tar clients.

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

`System.Formats.Tar.GnuTarEntry`  
`System.Formats.Tar.TarReader`  
`System.Formats.Tar.TarWriter`

* * *

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/45644:
### Description

This breaking change introduces default support for streaming HTTP responses in browser HTTP clients, which previously required opt-in. This enhancement leverages the capabilities of modern evergreen browsers to improve memory consumption and responsiveness, particularly for large data requests and scenarios like streaming AI chat responses. However, developers should be aware that the `response.Content.ReadAsStreamAsync()` method will now return a `BrowserHttpReadStream` instead of a `MemoryStream`, which does not support synchronous operations. This change may impact existing code that relies on synchronous stream operations.

See [dotnet/runtime#111680](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/111680) for details

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 3

### Previous behavior

In the Browser/Wasm/Blazor, previously the C# HTTP client buffered the whole response and the `HttpContent` contained full `MemoryStream` by default. Unless you opted in via `WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse` HttpRequestOption which enabled the streaming response, typical on other operating systems.

### New behavior

The `WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse` is now opt-out instead of opt-in. The `HttpContent` is not `MemoryStream` by default.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

To aid use-cases around streaming `GetFromJsonAsAsyncEnumerable` which work by default on other OSes.

### Recommended action

If you need to disable it globally, you can use

set env variable `DOTNET_WASM_ENABLE_STREAMING_RESPONSE`

We plan to make the `<WasmEnableStreamingResponse>false</WasmEnableStreamingResponse>` in your project file for in future previews of Net 10. See [dotnet/runtime#97449](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/97449)

To disable it for individual request, you can use existing  
`request.Options.Set(new HttpRequestOptionsKey<bool>("WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse"), false);`  
or `request.SetBrowserResponseStreamingEnabled(false)` extension in Blazor project

### Feature area

Networking

### Affected APIs

_No response_

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/45644:
### Description

This breaking change introduces default support for streaming HTTP responses in browser HTTP clients, which previously required opt-in. This enhancement leverages the capabilities of modern evergreen browsers to improve memory consumption and responsiveness, particularly for large data requests and scenarios like streaming AI chat responses. However, developers should be aware that the `response.Content.ReadAsStreamAsync()` method will now return a `BrowserHttpReadStream` instead of a `MemoryStream`, which does not support synchronous operations. This change may impact existing code that relies on synchronous stream operations.

See [dotnet/runtime#111680](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/111680) for details

### Version

.NET 10 Preview 3

### Previous behavior

In the Browser/Wasm/Blazor, previously the C# HTTP client buffered the whole response and the `HttpContent` contained full `MemoryStream` by default. Unless you opted in via `WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse` HttpRequestOption which enabled the streaming response, typical on other operating systems.

### New behavior

The `WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse` is now opt-out instead of opt-in. The `HttpContent` is not `MemoryStream` by default.

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

To aid use-cases around streaming `GetFromJsonAsAsyncEnumerable` which work by default on other OSes.

### Recommended action

If you need to disable it globally, you can use

set env variable `DOTNET_WASM_ENABLE_STREAMING_RESPONSE`

We plan to make the `<WasmEnableStreamingResponse>false</WasmEnableStreamingResponse>` in your project file for in future previews of Net 10. See [dotnet/runtime#97449](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/97449)

To disable it for individual request, you can use existing  
`request.Options.Set(new HttpRequestOptionsKey<bool>("WebAssemblyEnableStreamingResponse"), false);`  
or `request.SetBrowserResponseStreamingEnabled(false)` extension in Blazor project

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/42564:
### Description

[dotnet/runtime#80331](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/80331) introduced a minor breaking change only affecting malformed encoded payloads.

Prior to .NET 9, a malformed encoded string \[0x01, 0xC2\] parsed with BinaryReader.ReadString() would return an empty string.

ON .NET 9, it would return "\\uFFFD" which is the REPLACEMENT CHARACTER used to replace an unknown, unrecognised, or unrepresentable character. We accepted this change because it only affected malformed payloads and matches Unicode standards.

### Version

.NET 9 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // false
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 0
}

### New behavior

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // true
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 1
}

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Perf improvement affecting a rare scenario.

### Recommended action

If you want to keep the previous behavior where incomplete byte sequence were being omitted at the end of the string, you can TrimEnd("\\uFFFD") the result.

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

BinaryReader.ReadString()

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // false
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 0
}

### New behavior

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // true
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 1
}

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Perf improvement affecting a rare scenario.

### Recommended action

If you want to keep the previous behavior where incomplete byte sequence were being omitted at the end of the string, you can TrimEnd("\\uFFFD") the result.

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

BinaryReader.ReadString()

Contents of https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/42564:
### Description

[dotnet/runtime#80331](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/80331) introduced a minor breaking change only affecting malformed encoded payloads.

Prior to .NET 9, a malformed encoded string \[0x01, 0xC2\] parsed with BinaryReader.ReadString() would return an empty string.

ON .NET 9, it would return "\\uFFFD" which is the REPLACEMENT CHARACTER used to replace an unknown, unrecognised, or unrepresentable character. We accepted this change because it only affected malformed payloads and matches Unicode standards.

### Version

.NET 9 Preview 7

### Previous behavior

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // false
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 0
}

### New behavior

var ms \= new MemoryStream(new byte\[\] { 0x01, 0xC2 });
using (var br \= new BinaryReader(ms))
{
    string s \= br.ReadString();
    Console.WriteLine(s \== "\\uFFFD"); // true
    Console.WriteLine(s.Length); // 1
}

### Type of breaking change

*   **Binary incompatible**: Existing binaries might encounter a breaking change in behavior, such as failure to load or execute, and if so, require recompilation.
*   **Source incompatible**: When recompiled using the new SDK or component or to target the new runtime, existing source code might require source changes to compile successfully.
*   **Behavioral change**: Existing binaries might behave differently at run time.

### Reason for change

Perf improvement affecting a rare scenario.

### Recommended action

If you want to keep the previous behavior where incomplete byte sequence were being omitted at the end of the string, you can TrimEnd("\\uFFFD") the result.

### Feature area

Core .NET libraries

### Affected APIs

BinaryReader.ReadString()

data.content:
Based on my research of the .NET 11 Preview 1 breaking changes, here are the **four things that break** in your app:

---

### 1. GZipStream â€” empty streams now produce output
**[dotnet/runtime#118570](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/118570)**

Previously, if you wrote zero bytes to a `GZipStream` and disposed it, the underlying output stream had **0 bytes**. In .NET 11, it now writes valid gzip **headers and footers** even for empty input. Your "check if output is zero bytes" logic will **never detect the no-data case** anymore.

**Fix:** Check the *decompressed* length instead, or track whether you wrote data yourself.

---

### 2. TarReader â€” checksums are now validated
**[dotnet/runtime#118577](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/118577)** Â· **[dotnet/docs#49394](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49394)**

`TarReader.GetNextEntry()` now throws **`InvalidDataException`** on entries with invalid checksums. Previously it silently continued. Your hand-crafted legacy TAR files with bad checksums **will crash**.

**Fix:** Wrap reading in `try/catch (InvalidDataException)`, or fix the checksums in your legacy tool.

---

### 3. ZipArchive.CreateAsync â€” eager loading, no more lazy `.Entries`
**[dotnet/docs#50254](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/50254)** Â· **[dotnet/runtime#121938](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/121938)**

`CreateAsync` now **eagerly reads the central directory** (asynchronously). Exceptions from malformed entries now surface at `CreateAsync` time, not when you access `.Entries`. If your code catches errors on `.Entries` access or relies on lazy timing, that logic **moves to the `CreateAsync` call**.

**Fix:** Move error handling to the `await ZipArchive.CreateAsync(...)` call.

---

### 4. MemoryStream â€” lower max capacity, different exception type
**[dotnet/docs#49545](https://github.com/dotnet/docs/issues/49545)**

Max capacity changed from `int.MaxValue` (`0x7FFFFFFF`) to `Array.MaxLength` (`0x7FFFFFC7` â€” 56 bytes less). Setting capacity/length above the new limit now throws **`ArgumentOutOfRangeException`** instead of `OutOfMemoryException`.

**Fix:** Cap allocations at `Array.MaxLength`, and catch `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` in addition to (or instead of) `OutOfMemoryException`.
data.reasoningText:
Now I have the details on the GZipStream/DeflateStream change. The PR removed the `_wroteBytes` tracking and the conditional check in `PurgeBuffers`/`FlushBuffers`. Previously, if no data was written to the DeflateStream/GZipStream, the output would be zero bytes. Now, even with zero input, it writes out headers and footers (the GZIP header/trailer or DEFLATE block), so the output will be non-zero bytes.

Let me now summarize all four breaking changes for the user:

1. **GZipStream empty output**: In .NET 11, `DeflateStream`/`GZipStream`/`ZLibStream` now write out headers and footers even when no data is written. 

The shift here is that code relying on empty output to detect "no data written" will breakâ€”the streams now produce valid compressed output with headers and footers regardless of input.

2. **TarReader checksum validation**: In .NET 11, `TarReader` now validates checksums on TAR entries, throwing `InvalidDataException` for archives with invalid checksums instead of silently processing them.

3. **ZipArchive.CreateAsync eager loading**: In .NET 11, `ZipArchive.CreateAsync` eagerly reads all entries during the async call rather than deferring the load until `.Entries` is accessed.

4. **MemoryStream maximum capacity**: In .NET 11, `MemoryStream` enforces a maximum capacity of `0x7FFFFFC7` (Array.MaxLength) instead of `int.MaxValue`, and throws `ArgumentOutOfRangeException` instead of `OutOfMemoryException`.
